story:
After lunch on Montana, Sarah agreed to “browse” with her friend Annabelle, though she meant to resist buying anything. They wandered past the usual upscale stops until a bright blue door with stenciled flowers caught their eye. A brass sign read “The Secondhand Store.” In the window, a mannequin in an elegant suit held what looked like a Chanel purse. A secondhand shop on Montana seemed odd, but Annabelle, always ready for a bargain, led the way in.

Inside, an elderly saleslady in a neat knit suit welcomed them. Racks were sorted by size. Annabelle dug into the size fours, pulling out designer labels that still looked new. She marveled at a suit with tags still on and wondered why someone would give such things away. The saleslady explained gently that nothing had been “given away”—the former owners had died. She gestured at the size zeros: a model lost to anorexia. The size twos: an actress who overdosed. The size fours had come from a woman who died of cancer. Profits went to charity, she added, calling it a way for families to let go and for shoppers to get good clothes while doing some good. Sarah felt a chill at the thought of wearing the clothes of the recently dead, but Annabelle, reassured, headed for the dressing room with a sleek black suit.

While Annabelle tried things on, Sarah slipped into a cluttered back room stacked with old beaded bags, scarves, frames, and a box of discs. She idly flipped through until a cover made her stop. A girl in a gray turtleneck and jeans sat cross-legged on a bed, a guitar in her lap, one hand twisting a long brown ponytail. She looked exactly like Joanie, Sarah’s daughter away at Stanford. The room behind her even echoed Joanie’s—striped bedspread, cheerful wallpaper. The case had no title, only a small price sticker: $2. Inside was a DVD.

Annabelle emerged in the black suit, which fit like it had been made for her. She promised to brighten it with a scarf. Sarah bought the mysterious disc.

Back home, Sarah let out Buster, their golden retriever, checked email, and slid chicken breasts into the oven. Since Joanie had gone to college, she and Ben had fallen into the easy habit of eating in the den, watching old series with plates on their laps while Buster waited for dropped bites. Over a glass of wine, Sarah told Ben about the strange shop and showed him the DVD. “That’s eerie,” he said, looking at the cover. They put it on.

Bach played over a shot of the girl on the bed, guitar in hand, ponytail twist so familiar it hurt. She sang a soft, sad song about going away. Then she looked into the camera. “Hi Mom and Dad,” she said. She spoke as if she knew the room they were sitting in: plates, the dog begging. She said she’d hoped they’d find this before the news; that she hadn’t chosen to leave, but they shouldn’t worry; that she loved them and had been lucky to have them. The screen went to static.

Stunned, Sarah and Ben reached for their phones. Joanie’s went to voicemail. As they tried her dorm, the doorbell rang. A policewoman stood there with the news: that morning, on the way to San Francisco, Joanie had died in a car accident.

Annabelle wore the black suit to the funeral. The next day, The Secondhand Store was gone. No one they asked could remember ever seeing it.
